critic_raw:
This CNN vs. logistic regression cardiac risk prediction experiment has clean preprocessing discipline (normalization fit on training data only) and appropriate metric selection, but contains important structural problems. The split strategy uses 'stratified random sampling across all available years, holding out a random 15% of observations as the test set.' For a 5-year longitudinal cardiology dataset, random splitting allows patients recorded in year 1 and year 5 to be in the same training batch, violating temporal ordering. More importantly, cardiac risk prediction must generalize to future patients — a random split cannot evaluate this because it allows the model to train on 2024 ECG patterns and evaluate on 2020 ECGs, reversing the causal direction.

Second, the comparison is structurally asymmetric: the CNN receives raw 12-lead ECG waveforms as input (capturing subtle morphological patterns inaccessible to manual feature extraction), while the logistic regression baseline receives only clinical features (demographics, medications, risk factors) with no ECG-derived features at all. The design explicitly states this tests 'raw waveform analysis vs. non-waveform clinical data' — but this means a CNN improvement could be due to the information advantage of waveforms rather than deep learning's modeling superiority. A fair comparison would include an LR baseline trained on handcrafted ECG features (QT interval, ST elevation, PR interval, etc.) alongside the clinical features.

Third, the validation cohort description in Section 6 mentions 'year 4, ~2,000 patients' while the split strategy describes stratified random sampling across all years. These two descriptions are inconsistent — it is unclear whether the validation set is a specific year or a random stratified sample.

Finally, the CNN processes 12-lead ECGs but the lead configuration and acquisition protocol may vary across years or equipment upgrades — this between-period heterogeneity is not acknowledged as a confound.
all_issues_raised: Temporal validity: random stratified split across 5 years violates causal ordering for deployment — model can train on later-year ECGs and test on earlier-year ECGs
Confounded baseline comparison: CNN receives full ECG waveforms while LR receives only clinical features — information asymmetry confounds the deep learning vs. feature engineering comparison
Internal inconsistency: split strategy describes random stratified sampling while validation section specifies 'year 4' as the validation cohort — these are contradictory
ECG acquisition protocol heterogeneity across 5 years not addressed — differences in hardware, sampling rate, or lead placement create within-dataset distribution shift
No patient-level stratification: multiple ECGs from the same patient across years can appear in both training and test sets, inflating generalization estimates
Random temporal split for a 5-year cardiac dataset ignores secular trends in cardiac care, treatment standards, and outcome coding — a temporal split would better reflect deployment conditions

Logistic regression baseline has no access to ECG-derived features (QT interval, ST elevation) that are routinely available, conflating learned versus engineered waveform representation with data access asymmetry

Patient-level leakage risk: no specification of whether the split is at the patient level or recording level — if one patient has multiple recordings, the patient may appear in both train and test sets

The 10,000-patient dataset with 15% test holdout yields only 1,500 test patients; for a 12-month cardiac event rate that may be around 10–20%, this is 150–300 positive cases — potentially insufficient for precise AUROC estimation with subgroup analyses
No specified correction for multiple comparisons across secondary metric analyses (calibration, subgroup analyses by medication class), risking spurious findings in secondary reports

Random stratified split does not respect temporal ordering or patient-level independence — same patient can appear in both train and test sets across different years, creating patient-level leakage that inflates ECG-based performance estimates

Internal inconsistency: split strategy describes random 15% holdout, but model selection section references 'validation cohort (year 4)' — these are incompatible descriptions of the same dataset split

Comparison conflates feature modality difference (waveform vs. non-waveform) with model architecture difference (CNN vs. LR) — a hand-engineered ECG feature baseline would isolate the learned representation advantage

10,000 patients with 15% test holdout yields ~1,500 test patients — with multiple ECG events per patient possible, effective test-set patient count may be much lower, reducing statistical power for subgroup analyses

ECG preprocessing normalizes per-lead using training set mean and SD, but ECG amplitude varies substantially with patient body habitus and electrode placement — per-patient normalization might better align with clinical practice and reduce confounding
